mission.title: Install and Configure Kuma on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kuma is a modern Envoy-based service mesh that simplifies service connectivity, discovery, security, and observability across Kubernetes and VMs. Installing Kuma allows you to manage complex microservices architectures with ease, leveraging its multi-zone capabilities.

steps.title: Add the Kuma Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Kuma Helm repository to your Helm configuration:
```bash
helm repo add kuma https://kumahq.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kuma using Helm
steps.description:
Install Kuma with the following command, creating a namespace called 'kuma-system':
```bash
helm install kuma kuma/kuma --version v2.13.2 --namespace kuma-system --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Kuma pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kuma-system
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for the Kuma control plane
steps.description:
Forward the Kuma control plane port to access the dashboard:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/kuma-control-plane 5681:5681 --namespace kuma-system
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have the Kuma control plane running in the 'kuma-system' namespace, with all pods in the 'Running' state. You can access the Kuma dashboard via port forwarding.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kuma https://kumahq.github.io/charts
helm repo update
helm install kuma kuma/kuma --version v2.13.2 --namespace kuma-system --create-namespace

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Kuma release using Helm to remove the deployment from the cluster:
```bash
helm uninstall kuma --namespace kuma-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources associated with Kuma:
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep kuma.io | awk '{print $1}')
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the Kuma namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kuma-system
kubectl get namespaces | grep kuma
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of the Kuma resources:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace kuma-system -o yaml > kuma-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Kuma installation to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kuma kuma/kuma --version v2.13.3 --namespace kuma-system
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Kuma pods are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kuma-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If Kuma pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kuma-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Kuma control plane not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Kuma control plane, ensure the service is running:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kuma-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources allocated
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter resource allocation issues, check the resource limits set in the deployment:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment kuma-control-plane --namespace kuma-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails due to version conflicts, check the installed version and the available versions:
```bash
helm list --namespace kuma-system
helm search repo kuma/kuma --versions
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster and Helm installed to deploy Kuma.
